coredata.dc:title: Photoinduced synthesis of green photocatalyst Fe3O4/BiOBr/CQDs derived from corncob biomass for carbamazepine degradation: The role of selectively more CQDs decoration and Z-scheme structure
coredata.dc:creator: Xie, Xiaoyun
Li, Shan
Qi, Kemin
Wang, Zhaowei
coredata.dc:description: Seeking green, highly efficient and recyclable photocatalysts for emerging pollutants removal is urgent and desirable in water treatment area. In this work, novel and environmental-friendly photocatalyst Fe3O4/BiOBr/CQDs decorated by corncob biomass carbon quantum dots (CQDs) was fabricated successfully via photoinduced method for the first time. More CQDs as electrons reservoir were deposited on the (110) facet of BiOBr by photoinduced strategy compared to common hydrolysis method through morphology observation. Besides, enhanced visible light absorption and photogenerated charge transfer efficiency make Fe3O4/BiOBr/CQDs perform best photocatalytic activity towards carbamazepine (CBZ, 10 mg/L) removal, where 99.52% CBZ could be eliminated within 120 min light irradiation. Moreover, ·O2 –, h+ and ·OH dominated the photodegradation process and three detailed degradation pathways depicted the transformation behavior of CBZ, which were mainly attributed to Z-scheme structure construction caused by electronic bridging and up-conversion photoluminescence of biomass CQDs. A series of photoelectrochemical tests verified the existence of CQDs and Fe3O4 solved the high charge recombination rate and low electron utilization problems of BiOBr indeed. Combined with the rational utilization of corncob waste biomass, this research indicates green photocatalyst Fe3O4/BiOBr/CQDs synthesized by photoinduced method will have wide potential in water pollutant treatment in the future.

head.ce:title: Photoinduced synthesis of green photocatalyst Fe3O4/BiOBr/CQDs derived from corncob biomass for carbamazepine degradation: The role of selectively more CQDs decoration and Z-scheme structure

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Environmental Pollution Prediction and Control, Gansu Province, College of Earth and Environmental Sciences, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Xiaoyun Xie and Shan Li equally contributed to this work as co-first authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Seeking green, highly efficient and recyclable photocatalysts for emerging pollutants removal is urgent and desirable in water treatment area. In this work, novel and environmental-friendly photocatalyst Fe3O4/BiOBr/CQDs decorated by corncob biomass carbon quantum dots (CQDs) was fabricated successfully via photoinduced method for the first time. More CQDs as electrons reservoir were deposited on the (110) facet of BiOBr by photoinduced strategy compared to common hydrolysis method through morphology observation. Besides, enhanced visible light absorption and photogenerated charge transfer efficiency make Fe3O4/BiOBr/CQDs perform best photocatalytic activity towards carbamazepine (CBZ, 10 mg/L) removal, where 99.52% CBZ could be eliminated within 120 min light irradiation. Moreover, ·O2 –, h+ and ·OH dominated the photodegradation process and three detailed degradation pathways depicted the transformation behavior of CBZ, which were mainly attributed to Z-scheme structure construction caused by electronic bridging and up-conversion photoluminescence of biomass CQDs. A series of photoelectrochemical tests verified the existence of CQDs and Fe3O4 solved the high charge recombination rate and low electron utilization problems of BiOBr indeed. Combined with the rational utilization of corncob waste biomass, this research indicates green photocatalyst Fe3O4/BiOBr/CQDs synthesized by photoinduced method will have wide potential in water pollutant treatment in the future.

ce:section.ce:para: The environmental problems caused by emerging pollutants such as Pharmaceuticals and Personal Care Products (PPCPs) have been paid much attention in recent years [1]. Thereinto, carbamazepine (CBZ) as the representative central pain analgesic, has played positive role in treating trigeminal neuralgia, epilepsy or other mental diseases, yet has also brought negative effects to the environment [2–4]. CBZ residuals existed in the surface water, underground water and drinking water would not bring acute toxicity due to low exposure levels (ng/L-µg/L), however, chronic toxicity to creatures was confirmed in the reported literature [3,5]. For example, the mRNA level of mussel gill tissue of invertebrates in water could be significantly increased after 1 day of CBZ exposure, while the mRNA levels of superoxide dismutase and metallothionein of digestive gland gills were decreased under CBZ exposure for a long time, showing the obvious inhibition effect [6]. Hard biodegradation property makes CBZ persistent and bioaccumulative due to the unique chemical structure of symmetrical aromatic heterocycle [7]. Thus, how to effectively eliminate CBZ residuals in aqueous is a hot topic to be discussed and solved by researchers.

Semiconductor photocatalysis technology as one of the Advanced Oxidation Processes (AOPs) can effectively degrade refractory organic pollutants because of its strong oxidation capacity, fast reaction rate, easy operation and less secondary pollution [8–10]. Bismuth oxybromide (BiOBr) as the novel visible-light-response bismuth-based photocatalyst is consisted of [Bi2O2]2+ layers and double slabs of bromide atoms, which has attracted much attention and interest due to its moderate band gap energy (∼2.6 eV), outstanding optical property and excellent stability [11–13]. Besides, a few reported researches pointed out (110) facet of BiOBr exhibited higher photocatalytic activity because of its greater surface energy and stronger internal electric field [11,14]. However, photogenerated charge carriers recombination and low photocatalytic efficiency are the vital challenges to restrict the practical application of BiOBr [15,16]. Recently, interfacial regulation and constructing heterojunction have been considered as efficient strategies for the activity optimization of semiconductor photocatalysts [17–19].

Notably, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), novel “zero-dimensional” carbon-based nanomaterials, are considered to be outstanding candidates of interfacial regulation due to their intrinsic natures of up-conversion photoluminescence, excellent electron-transfer ability and good biocompatibility [20,21]. There are many sources for preparing CQDs, while hydrothermal preparation of CQDs derived from waste biomass is a more environmental-friendly method than that from chemical reagents such as ethylenediamine and citric acid [22,23]. Moreover, how to perform excellent synergetic role between the electron transfer ability of biomass CQDs and the crystal surface characteristics of BiOBr is a challenge worth further consideration. Using common impregnation or adsorption methods can result in random distribution and unstable binding of CQDs, while some researches demonstrated that photogenerated electrons and holes of photocatalyst with strong redox ability could be distributed spatially to different crystal planes under light irradiation [24–26]. Therefore, we suppose that light-driven synthesis method such as photodeposition method is an innovative choice for biomass CQDs as electron acceptors to be more completely and firmly distributed on the crystal planes of BiOBr, and the experimental results in this research confirmed our prediction.

Furthermore, constructing magnetic bismuth-based heterojunction photocatalyst can not only settle with the problem of photogenerated charge recombination, but also promote the separation and regeneration abilities with the help of external magnetic field for practical application [27,28]. Common magnetic materials mainly include ferrites (MnFe2O4, CoFe2O4, ZnFe2O4), metal oxides (Fe3O4, Co3O4, Mn3O4, γ-Fe2O3), and pure metals (Fe0, Co) [29–31]. Thereinto, Fe3O4 as the representative iron oxide is widely used to form bismuth-based/magnetic heterojunction photocatalysts due to its intrinsic benefits of good stability, strong magnetism and low cost [32,33]. It owns inverse spinel structure Fe3+[Fe2+Fe3+]O4 2-, and its interaction between Fe2+ in octahedral sites and Fe3+ in tetrahedral sites is beneficial for the strong magnetism [34]. Meanwhile, the intrinsic n-type semiconductor property of Fe3O4 is good for photogenerated charge transfer [35]. Therefore, combining it with BiOBr can solve the difficult problems of nanoparticles recovery and photogenerated charge recombination.

Herein, inspired by photodeposition method and environmental-friendly strategy, constructing novel magnetic biomass CQDs-based photocatalyst Fe3O4/BiOBr/CQDs via photoinduced method to photodegrade CBZ is the object of the research. Firstly, corncob biomass CQDs and novel heterojunction Fe3O4/BiOBr/CQDs were fabricated successfully by hydrothermal and photoinduced methods. Secondly, the differences between photoinduced synthesis and common hydrolysis synthesis on crystal type, crystal morphology, light absorption ability and catalytic activity of photocatalyst Fe3O4/BiOBr/CQDs were emphatically studied. Thirdly, considering the practical application, the properties of Fe3O4/BiOBr/CQDs were investigated from the aspects of material proportion optimization, common influencing factors of natural water, regeneration and stability of photocatalyst. Moreover, the degradation activity on CBZ using the photocatalyst Fe3O4/BiOBr/HC produced by corncob charcoal residue during the synthesis of CQDs was also discussed. Lastly, according to characterization and experimental results, detailed and plausible CBZ degradation mechanism and pathways by Fe3O4/BiOBr/CQDs were proposed.

Waste corncob biomass from Yuzhong county, Lanzhou City of China was applied as biomass carbon quantum dots precursor. Bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O), sodium bromide (NaBr) and ferroferric oxide (Fe3O4) were all analytical grades. Benzoquinone (BQ), isopropyl alcohol (IPA), and ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA) with analytical grades were used as scavengers of superoxide radicals, hydroxyl radicals, and photogenerated holes, respectively. Target pollutant CBZ was produced by J&K Scientific Company of China and the related parameters were listed in Table S1. The mobile phase methanol and ultrapure water which were used to detect CBZ concentration were HPLC grades.

Corncob biomass CQDs were fabricated by hydrothermal method. 0.5 g corncob biomass was dispersed into 10 mL ultrapure water with magnetic stirring for 1 h. Then, it was poured into 30 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 ℃ for 3 h. When cooling to room temperature, the orange suspension was collected and subjected to dialysis for 24 h. The obtained CQDs were stored under the dark condition with low temperature (4 ℃). Meanwhile, in consideration of green and environmental-friendly strategy, the remaining corncob biomass hydrothermal charcoal residues (HC) after drying were collected for the subsequent use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Fe3O4/BiOBr/CQDs (L), Fe3O4/BiOBr/CQDs (NL) and Fe3O4/BiOBr/HC
ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalyst Fe3O4/BiOBr/CQDs (L) was prepared using photoinduced method under light irradiation. Typically, 0.5 mmol Bi(NO3)3·5H2O was completely dissolved into 20 mL ethylene glycol and it was denoted as solution A. Besides, the suspension concluding 0.02 g Fe3O4 nanoparticles with 20 mL distilled water was marked as solution B. Then, the above solution A and B were mixed thoroughly and a certain volume of CQDs solution was added. Next, 50 W energy-saving visible LED light (light intensity: 252.2 mW/cm2; luminous flux: 3285.16 lm) was turned on and 0.05 M NaBr solution (20 mL) was added dropwise to above suspension with stirring for 6 h. The produced gray-blue precipitation was taken out and purified for 3 times with ethanol and distilled water. After drying at 60 ℃ in an air drying oven, the target products were stored in a closed, dry and dark environment. The obtained photocatalysts Fe3O4/BiOBr/CQDs (L) with 0.5 mL, 1 mL, 2 mL, 4 mL and 6 mL CQDs solution were labeled as FeBrCQDs-0.5, FeBrCQDs-1, FeBrCQDs-2, FeBrCQDs-4 and FeBrCQDs-6, respectively.

Photocatalyst Fe3O4/BiOBr/CQDs (NL) was prepared using the above similar method but under no light irradiation. Meanwhile, the adding volume of CQDs solution was 4 mL and the final product was denoted as FeBrCQDs-4 (NL). Furthermore, BiOBr and Fe3O4/BiOBr photocatalysts were synthesized via same photoinduced method but we need to change certain chemical amounts: BiOBr (with no CQDs and Fe3O4); Fe3O4/BiOBr (with no CQDs).

Photocatalyst Fe3O4/BiOBr/HC was fabricated by the modified one-step hydrolysis method according to our previous reported research [36]. Notably, the method difference was that 10% biochar was replaced by 10% hydrothermal charcoal residues, and the obtained final product Fe3O4/BiOBr/HC was stored in dry and closed environment.
Scheme 1 presented the specific and detailed fabrication procedures of materials, and emphasized the rational utilization of waste resources.

Surface morphologies of obtained products were detected through field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM) (FEI Apreo S, Thermo Scientific, USA). Elementary composition and distribution of photocatalysts were determined using energy dispersive X-ray spectrum-mapping (EDS-mapping) and EDS (IE250, Oxford Instruments, UK). The microstructures of photocatalysts Fe3O4/BiOBr/CQDs (L) and Fe3O4/BiOBr/CQDs (NL) were analyzed via field emission transmission electron microscope (TEM) (FEI TECNAI G2, USA; FEI Tecnai F30, Netherlands). The crystal structure and crystallinity of samples were determined through X-ray diffractometer (XRD) (D/max-2400, Rigaku, Japan). Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) parameters of products were provided via N2 adsorption–desorption surface area instrument (Tristar II 3020, USA). Magnetic hysteresis loop (VSM) of FeBrCQDs-4 was depicted via a magnetometer (Lakeshore 735, USA). Chemical state and composition of as-prepared samples were determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, AXIS Ultra DLD, Shimadzu, Japan). Surficial functional groups of samples were observed by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Nexus 670, Thermo-Nicolet, USA). UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS) (Carry 5000, Agilent, USA) was applied to evaluate the optical absorption property of different photocatalysts. Photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra and nanosecond-level time-resolved fluorescence decay dynamics of BiOBr, Fe3O4/BiOBr and FeBrCQDs-4 were investigated through a steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer (FLSP920, Edinburgh Instruments, UK). Excitation-dependent PL spectra of corncob biomass CQDs were identified by a Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, USA). Zeta potentials of FeBrCQDs-4 at various pH were studied through Zeta Potential Analyzer (NanoBrook 90Plus PALS, Brookhaven, USA). The superoxide (·O2 –) and hydroxyl radicals (·OH) were investigated using 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) spin-trapping agent by electron spin resonance (ESR) spectrometer (Bruker A300-9.5/12, Germany). Leaching of iron and bismuth ions existing in final reaction solution was evaluated through high resolution inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometer (ICP-OES, Plasma Quant PQ 9000, Analytik Jena, Germany).

Photoelectrochemical tests of photocatalysts were carried out through CS350H electrochemical workstation (Wuhan CorrTest Instruments Corp. Ltd.). Open circuit potential (OCP), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), photocurrent response (PCR) and Mott-Schottky plot (M−S) were employed under standard three-electrode system: working electrode-indium-tin oxide sheet glass (ITO); reference electrode-Ag/AgCl; counter electrode-platinum wires. The utilization of electrolyte solution was as follows: EIS and M−S measurements-1 mM K3[Fe(CN)6] solution; OCP and PCR measurements-0.1 M Na2SO4 solution. 50 W visible LED lamp was chosen to be light source and all photoelectrochemical tests were studied at room temperature.

CBZ photodegradation experiments were conducted under 50 W visible LED light irradiation. Specifically, visible LED lamp (dominant wavelength: 475 nm, detailed wavelength range was shown in Fig. S1) was at the distance of 5 cm from the reaction beaker containing 0.03 g photocatalyst Fe3O4/BiOBr/CQDs (L) and CBZ solution (10 mg/L, 50 mL). Prior to photodegradation, the reaction suspension was stirred in dark for 30 min to get adsorption/desorption equilibrium. Then, the light source was turned on and the samples were collected at certain time intervals. Next, they should be removed photocatalysts using 0.22 µm membrane filters and filtered into 2 mL liquid chromatography vials for further analysis. Above experiments were carried out in duplicates and the calculated mean values were as the final data.

Notably, the photocatalytic performances of various photocatalysts (Fe3O4/BiOBr/CQDs (NL), Fe3O4/BiOBr, BiOBr) were evaluated under the same above reaction conditions. Meanwhile, material ratio optimization (CQDs: 0.5 mL, 1 mL, 2 mL, 4 mL and 6 mL; Fe3O4: 0.01 g, 0.02 g and 0.04 g), effects of photocatalyst dosage (0.2 g/L–1.0 g/L) and initial solution pH (3.0–9.0) on photocatalytic activity were studied.

CBZ concentration was analyzed through ultrahigh performance liquid chromatography (UHPLC, Ultimate 3000, Dionex, USA) with Accucore XL C18 column (250 mm × 4.6 mm, 5 µm) and RS diode array detector. Detailed analytical conditions were as follows: Mobile phase constitutions: 80% methanol and 20% ultrapure water; retention time: 5 min; flow rate: 0.6 mL/min; column compartment temperature: 30 °C; UV detection wavelength: 283 nm.

Main CBZ photodegradation products were analyzed by high resolution mass spectrometry (HRMS, Orbitrap Elite, Velos Pro, Thermo Scientific, USA) with positive electrospray ionization mode (100–800 m/z). In addition, negative ionization mode (100–400 m/z) was used to identify ring-rupturing intermediates. Total organic carbon (TOC) of reaction solution was tested by TOC analyzer (VarioTOC, Elementar Analysensysteme GmbH, Germany).

Morphology and element distribution of as-prepared photocatalysts were analyzed by SEM and EDS-mapping images as presented in Fig. 1 . Photocatalyst BiOBr presented thin sheet shape and magnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles mainly exhibited nanosphere morphology (Fig. 1(a and b)). Fe3O4 nanoparticles were dispersed on the interface of BiOBr when the above two materials were composed together to form Fe3O4/BiOBr (Fig. 1(c)). Besides, similar hierarchy morphologies were also observed on the ternary composites FeBrCQDs-4 (NL) (Fig. 1(d)) and FeBrCQDs-4 (Fig. 1(e)), demonstrating the successful loading of Fe3O4. Notably, no CQDs with particle size less than 10 nm were found due to the limited resolution of SEM and higher resolution electron microscopy such as TEM was needed, however, the presence of C, N and O elements was detected based on EDS-mapping analysis (Fig. 1(f)), which proved the successful introduction of CQDs in FeBrCQDs-4. Moreover, the Bi, O, Br, Fe, C and N components of FeBrCQDs-4 (NL) (Fig. 1(g)) and FeBrCQDs-4 (Fig. 1(h)) were determined by EDS analysis and detailed element ratios data were listed in Table S2. Interestingly, weight ratios of element C (23.97%) and N (21.21%) of FeBrCQDs-4 were improved obviously compared with those of FeBrCQDs-4 (NL), meaning that photoinduced method was more conducive to the introduction of corncob biomass CQDs, where photons could induce the conduction band and valence band of photocatalyst BiOBr to produce photogenerated electrons and holes, and the formed internal electric field attracted CQDs as electron acceptors to gather on the surface of BiOBr [11,37,38].

To further explore morphologies of CQDs and observe the distribution of CQDs onto the surfaces of BiOBr, TEM images of CQDs, FeBrCQDs-4 (NL) and FeBrCQDs-4 were obtained as presented in Fig. S2 and Fig. 2 . Clearly, black dots could be observed and the size of corncob biomass CQDs was about 5–7 nm as shown in Fig. S2(a, b). Besides, the (100) facets of graphite carbon could be observed in the high-resolution TEM images (Fig. S2(c, d)), which demonstrated the successful preparation of CQDs [39]. Fe3O4 nanoparticles assembled with sheet-like BiOBr could be found from the TEM images of FeBrCQDs-4 (NL), but CQDs were hardly seen and only a few (100) facets of graphite carbon existed in CQDs were discovered through high resolution TEM images Fig. 2(a-d). However, in contrast, many CQDs could be discovered on the surface of BiOBr for photocatalyst FeBrCQDs-4 (Fig. 2(e-g)). Besides, the lattice spacing of 0.28 nm, 0.25 nm and 0.22 nm could be indexed to (110), (311) and (100) facets of BiOBr, Fe3O4 and CQDs, respectively (Fig. 2(h)), which confirmed the excellent combination of above three kinds of materials. Compared with TEM characterization results of two photocatalysts FeBrCQDs-4 (NL) and FeBrCQDs-4 synthesized without light irradiation and with light irradiation, it could be concluded that CQDs with excellent electron transfer ability would be more likely to be deposited on the (110) facet with strong internal electric field of BiOBr under light irradiation, further proving the hypothesis mentioned in the introduction.

The phase structure and crystallinity of BiOBr, Fe3O4, Fe3O4/BiOBr, FeBrCQDs-4 (NL) and Fe3O4/BiOBr/CQDs (L) with various amounts of CQDs were studied by XRD as shown in Fig. 3 (a and b). The obtained photocatalysts were consistent with the crystal type of tetragonal BiOBr (JCPDS No. 09-0393), where the typical diffraction peaks at 10.90°, 31.69°, 32.22°, 46.21° and 57.12° were corresponded to (001), (102), (110), (220) and (212) planes, respectively. Meanwhile, the (311), (400), (511) and (440) facets of cubic crystal phase Fe3O4 (JCPDS No.19–0629) could also be found in the XRD patterns of Fe3O4/BiOBr, FeBrCQDs-4 (NL) and Fe3O4/BiOBr/CQDs (L) with various amounts of CQDs, indicating the successful combination of magnetic Fe3O4 nanoparticles. Nevertheless, no diffraction peak of CQDs was found in the XRD patterns of Fe3O4/BiOBr/CQDs (L), which might be due to low introduction content and poor crystallinity of CQDs [40]. Notably, the diffraction peak intensity of FeBrCQDs-4 was higher obviously than that of FeBrCQDs-4 (NL), illustrating Fe3O4/BiOBr/CQDs synthesized by photoinduced method had better crystallinity and high catalytic activity potential. Furthermore, with the introduction of various contents of CQDs, the crystal intensity of Fe3O4/BiOBr/CQDs (L) changed and FeBrCQDs-4 exhibited the best crystal structure, especially in (110) facet, which proved that the deposition of CQDs on (110) facet had a certain influence on the crystallinity of BiOBr.

To further investigate the surficial chemical composition and electronic state of FeBrCQDs-4, XPS analysis was conducted as presented in Fig. 3(c-i). The wide spectrum verified Br, Bi, O, C, N and Fe elements of each unitary sample coexisted in the composite photocatalyst FeBrCQDs-4 (Fig. 3(c)). Besides, the high-resolution XPS spectra of above elements were shown in Fig. 3(d-i). Specifically, binding energies of 157.1 eV, 162.4 eV and 66.6 eV were corresponded to two Bi 4f characteristic peaks and one Br 3d characteristic peak of FeBrCQDs-4, respectively. The characteristic peaks of O 1s were located at 528.0 eV and 530.1 eV, which were O2– ions of BiOBr and Fe3O4 and oxygenic functional groups of CQDs respectively [41,42]. Binding energies of 708.4 eV and 721.8 eV were related to characteristic peaks of Fe 2p, representing Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions respectively [43]. While the characteristic peaks of C 1s existed in the binding energies of 282.5 eV, 285.1 eV and 286.5 eV, where the characteristic peaks of 285.1 eV and 286.5 eV were corresponded to the C–C bond and oxygen-containing carbon of graphene [44,45], proving the existence of CQDs. The peak of N 1s existed in CQDs at binding energy of 397.3 eV was also discovered. Notably, changes in the electron density could be reflected by the shifts of binding energy in the XPS spectra [46]. On one hand, the obvious red shifts of Bi 4f, O 1s and Br 3d binding energies and blue shifts of C 1s binding energy could be observed after incorporating CQDs, which were ascribed to the fact that CQDs as electrons bridge accepted the transfer of electrons. On the other hand, no significant shift in Fe 2p and O 1s binding energies of FeBrCQDs-4 compared to Fe3O4 was determined, inferring that the electrons transfer occurs mainly through BiOBr and CQDs rather than Fe3O4 and further proving the possibility of Z-scheme photocatalytic mechanism [47,48].

Table S3 listed the basic BET parameters of as-prepared photocatalysts. Compared to pristine BiOBr and binary Fe3O4/BiOBr, FeBrCQDs-4 decorated by corncob biomass CQDs owned larger specific surface area (22.03 m2 g−1) and micropore volume (3.259 × 10-3 cm3 g−1), which increased the contact opportunity with pollutant CBZ molecules and thus promoted photocatalytic degradation process. Besides, Fig. 4 (a) depicts the chemical bonds and functional groups of samples through FTIR analysis. CQDs contained rich functional groups and chemical bonds, where the stretching vibration peaks at 1562 cm−1, 3421 cm−1 and 1740–1600 cm−1 were assigned to NH adsorption band, OH functional group and oxidative functional groups (i.e. carbonyl), respectively [49,50]. The sharp peak at 3008–2761 cm−1 was assigned to CH2 and CH bonds, and the back edge of the peak was highly stretched, indicating that it was rich in carboxylic acid groups [49]. For composite photocatalyst FeBrCQDs-4, Bi-O (515 cm−1 and 798 cm−1), O-Br (1039 cm−1), Fe-O (580 cm−1) and NH (1562 cm−1) stretching vibration peaks occurred, indicating its fabrication succeeded [51,52]. Moreover, the presence of defective oxygen at 1091 cm−1 was also observed [53], referring a strong interaction between CQDs and BiOBr and Fe3O4.

Additionally, magnetism property of FeBrCQDs-4 was also explored via VSM as shown in Fig. 4(b). Saturation magnetization (Ms) value and coerce value of FeBrCQDs-4 were 7.1 emu/g and 30.50 Oe, respectively, indicating the good paramagnetic characteristic and difficulty to demagnetization, which was conducive to the long-term recovery from the wastewater.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical and photoelctrochemical properties of photocatalysts
ce:section.ce:para: Optical and photoelctrochemical properties are vital indexes to evaluate the photocatalytic performance of photocatalyst. UV–Vis diffuse reflectance absorption spectra (DRS) of photocatalysts were shown in Fig. 5 (a). Pristine BiOBr presented strong response in ultraviolet and near-visible-light regions, and the absorption edge was about 450 nm. After introduction of Fe3O4 and CQDs, the optical absorption intensity in visible-light part improved, especially for FeBrCQDs-4. By comparison, FeBrCQDs-4 with more CQDs synthesized by photoinduced method exhibited the best optical absorption ability among BiOBr, Fe3O4/BiOBr and FeBrCQDs-4 (NL), which indicated that CQDs with excellent up-conversion fluorescence performance and light absorption ability promoted the light capturing ability of composite photocatalyst FeBrCQDs-4 [54]. Meanwhile, based on Kubelka-Munk function [55], the band gaps (Eg) of BiOBr, Fe3O4, Fe3O4/BiOBr, FeBrCQDs-4 (NL) and FeBrCQDs-4 were calculated as 2.51 eV, 1.61 eV, 2.19 eV, 1.80 eV and 1.31 eV, respectively, which further proved that FeBrCQDs-4 was more easily excited by visible light (Fig. 5(b)).

The excitation-dependent PL behavior of corncob biomass CQDs was confirmed as exhibited in Fig. S3. Some references pointed out that this complex behavior was attributed to the surface state affecting the band gap of CQDs and the size effect caused by quantum dimensions [56,57]. Besides, photoluminescence (PL) emission spectra and time-resolved fluorescence decay spectra of BiOBr, Fe3O4/BiOBr and FeBrCQDs-4 were used to investigate the charge transfer of photocatalysts (Fig. 5(c and d)). Weaker PL intensity and longer decay lifetime mean lower charge recombination rate and faster charge transfer [33]. Obviously, FeBrCQDs-4 owned the weakest PL intensity and longest PL decay lifetime, elucidating that the decoration of CQDs significantly decreased the possibility of photogenerated holes-electrons recombination and promoted the charge transfer so that enhanced the photocatalytic performance.

Furthermore, photoelectrochemical experiments including EIS, OCP and PCR were conducted to further investigate the photogenerated charge transfer ability of FeBrCQDs-4. As depicted in Fig. 6 (a), among the EIS spectra of BiOBr, Fe3O4/BiOBr and FeBrCQDs-4, smaller arc radius of FeBrCQDs-4 indicated the higher charge transfer efficiency due to the incorporating of CQDs and Fe3O4. Meanwhile, the OCP difference of Fe3O4/BiOBr and FeBrCQDs-4 in dark and light was exhibited in the inset figure. Higher △OCP value (0.029 V) of FeBrCQDs-4 suggested the more intense band bending, which might be attributed to the greater Fermi level pinning effect from the special surface state of CQDs [58]. Moreover, PCR results also demonstrated the pronounced charge separation ability of FeBrCQDs-4 (Fig. 6(b)), and its higher photocurrent density indicated the existence of CQDs and Fe3O4 solved the high charge recombination rate and low electron utilization problems of BiOBr indeed. To explore the subsequent photocatalytic mechanism, another electrochemical test (M−S plot) of BiOBr and Fe3O4 was conducted as depicted in Fig. 6(c and d). Based on the fundamental calculation (See Supporting Information Text S1), on one hand, both positive slopes of plots indicated the above two materials were n-type semiconductors; on the other hand, the flat band potentials (Vfb) of BiOBr and Fe3O4 were −0.36 V vs. Ag/AgCl and −0.43 V vs. Ag/AgCl respectively (corresponding to −0.16 V vs. NHE and −0.23 V vs. NHE respectively), where the relevant conduction band potentials could be calculated as −0.26 V vs. NHE and −0.33 V vs. NHE, respectively. Therefore, the valence band potentials of BiOBr and Fe3O4 were 2.25 V vs. NHE and 1.28 V vs. NHE, respectively.

To evaluate the photocatalytic activity of above photocatalysts, a series of CBZ degradation experiments were conducted. Firstly, the photocatalytic performance comparison of photocatalysts synthesized by two different methods (photoinduced method and hydrolysis method) was shown in Fig. 7 (a). Photoinduced method had little influence on CBZ degradation by pristine photocatalysts (BiOBr (NL) and BiOBr) and binary photocatalysts (Fe3O4/BiOBr (NL) and Fe3O4/BiOBr). However, after the incorporating of CQDs, the benefit of photoinduced method emerged due to the excellent light absorption and electrons transfer abilities. FeBrCQDs-4 performed the perfect photocatalytic activity, where the removal rate of CBZ could reach 99.52% within 120 min, proving that the combination of photoinduced method and the existence of CQDs greatly enhanced the catalytic activity of photocatalyst. Besides, material ratios optimization tests were also studied as shown in Fig. 7(b). When the volume of CQDs was 4 mL and the amount of Fe3O4 was 0.02 g, the photocatalytic activity of Fe3O4/BiOBr/CQDs was highest. The activity would be inhibited with adding low or high CQDs contents, which might be because insufficient active sites or excessive CQDs affected the crystal surface activity of BiOBr and limited the light absorption of photocatalyst [59]. At the same time, appropriate Fe3O4 content can improve the photogenic charge separation rate, thus promoting the degradation efficiency [60]. Furthermore, compared with BiOBr and Fe3O4/BiOBr, FeBrCQDs-4 presented the best degradation efficiency of CBZ (99.52%) and pseudo first-order rate constant k (0.03807 min−1) (Fig. 7(c)), which was ascribed to synergistic effect of Fe3O4, CQDs and BiOBr.

In view of the concept of green and environmental protection, we also explored the photocatalytic activities of other four kinds of biomass Fe3O4/BiOBr/CQDs (apple tree branches: A-FeBrCQDs, reed straw: R-FeBrCQDs, potato straw: P-FeBrCQDs, broccoli root: B-FeBrCQDs) and corncob hydrothermal charcoal-based photocatalyst Fe3O4/BiOBr/HC (Fig. 7(d)). Interestingly, the removal efficiencies of CBZ using the above photocatalysts were 98.97%, 98.28%, 88.40%, 79.88% and 99.05%, respectively, which were higher than that using pure BiOBr (68.89%). On one hand, these results indicated it was universal and adaptable for photoinduced method and CQDs modified strategy on CBZ degradation using Fe3O4/BiOBr/CQDs; on the other hand, the residual hydrothermal carbon residue was also a promising material, which was worthy of further research and exploration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of dosage and initial solution pH on CBZ photodegradation
ce:section.ce:para: In practical applications, dosage of photocatalyst and initial pH of reaction solution are major influencing factors for photocatalysts. Fig. S4 presented the related effects on CBZ photodegradation. 0.6 g/L dosage exhibited best CBZ removal efficiency of 99.52% within 120 min light irradiation (Fig. S4(a)), where excessive or insufficient dosages caused light shielding and scattering effects or reactive sites limitation, thus blocking the degradation process [61]. As exhibited in Fig. S4(b), the CBZ removal rates at pH 5.0–9.0 were 96.41%, 99.52%, 97.54% and 99.18% within 120 min irradiation, respectively, indicating FeBrCQDs-4 had high photocatalytic activity on CBZ degradation in weak acidic, neutral and alkaline environment. When pH = 3.0, the removal efficiency decreased to 83.57%, which might be due to the hyperacidity condition affecting the production of active species ·O2 – and ·OH [62]. Besides, zeta potentials of FeBrCQDs-4 at various pH were determined as given in Table S4. The pH of zero point charge (pHzpc) for FeBrCQDs-4 was speculated between 5.0 and 6.0, while some references reported two pKa values of CBZ (pKa1 = 2.3, pKa2 = 13.9) [63,64], which illustrated electrostatic attraction and repulsion existed on the surface of pollutant molecules and materials all the time due to the existence of amphoteric ions and thus pH had little influence on the reaction system.

Photocatalyst FeBrCQDs-4 could be quickly separated from aqueous solution through magnets. To consider the possibility of practical application, the regeneration performance and stability were discussed in detail and the results were plotted in Fig. S5. After five rounds of recycling experiment, FeBrCQDs-4 showed excellent catalytic activity stability and maintained high photocatalytic activity. The degradation removal rates of CBZ in the first five rounds of recycling experiment were 99.52%, 99.36%, 97.67%, 98.23% and 89.08%, respectively. Bismuth and iron ions leaching of final solution were also detected by high resolution inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometer (ICP-OES, Plasma Quant PQ 9000, Analytik Jena, Germany) and related data were shown in Table S5. Calculated dissolution rates of Bi and Fe ions were 0.000275% and 0%, respectively. Meanwhile, it could be clearly seen that the XRD diffraction peaks of FeBrCQDs-4 involved in photocatalysis did not change significantly compared with that before photocatalysis, which further verified that the FeBrCQDs-4 had good structural stability.

Generally, active species play vital and irreplaceable roles in photocatalytic reaction process. In this study, isopropanol (IPA), ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA) and benzoquinone (BQ) were selected as scavengers of hydroxyl (·OH), photogenerated hole (h+) and superoxide radical (·O2 –), respectively, and the results were shown in Fig. 8 (a). Without any scavengers, after illumination for 120 min, the removal rate of CBZ by photocatalyst FeBrCQDs-4 was 99.52%. However, after adding the above reagents, the activities of CBZ photodegradation were all inhibited, where the removal efficiencies were 77.80%, 15.17% and 0.32%, respectively, revealing ·OH, h+ and ·O2 – were all involved in the CBZ degradation process. And the inhibition of BQ was most obvious, which elucidated that the high removal efficiency of CBZ by FeBrCQDs-4 under visible LED light irradiation was mainly attributed to ·O2 –. Meanwhile, the existence of DMPO-·O2 – and DMPO-·OH signals also demonstrated the production of ·O2 – and ·OH in the reaction system by ESR spectra (Fig. 8(b))

Combined with characterization, photoelctrochemical results and the role of active species, the plausible degradation mechanism of FeBrCQDs-4 on CBZ degradation under visible LED light irradiation was proposed as depicted in Scheme 2 . Conventional type-II photocatalytic mechanism couldn’t well explain the production of ·OH and ·O2 – radicals because the conduction band (CB) potential of BiOBr and valence band (VB) potential of Fe3O4 couldn’t meet the potentials of O2/·O2 – and H2O/·OH. However, Z-scheme photocatalytic mechanism was more convincing and plausible. Under visible LED light irradiation, the electrons (e-) on the VB of Fe3O4 and BiOBr were exited and transited to the CB so that the photogenerated holes (h+) were formed on the VB. Then, biomass CQDs as the receptor and donor [65] of electrons promoted the transfer of electrons on the CB of BiOBr (ECB = -0.26 eV) to the VB of Fe3O4 (EVB = 1.28 eV). Therefore, the e- on the CB of Fe3O4 (ECB = -0.33 eV) and the h+ on the VB of BiOBr (EVB = 2.25 eV) could maintain strong oxidation reducibility and improve the catalytic activity of photocatalyst. On one hand, O2 in the aqueous environment could react with e- to form ·O2 –, and further oxidize pollutant CBZ to be degraded; on the other hand, h+ would directly mineralize CBZ or react with H2O to generate ·OH to remove CBZ. Besides, in addition to promoting photogenerated charge transfer as electron acceptor and donor, CQDs also played a role in improving visible light capturing. When excited by visible light, the e- in the π orbital of CQDs could jump to the high energy π* orbital, and then the excited e- would return to the low energy σ orbital due to radiation relaxation, facilitating the emission of short wavelength light [66,67]. Thus, visible light could be fully utilized to fundamentally enhance the light absorption and photocatalytic activity of photocatalyst Fe3O4/BiOBr/CQDs.

CBZ degradation products were determined through HRMS analysis and three probable degradation pathways of CBZ were provided as shown in Scheme 3 . In pathway I, ·O2 – and h+ attacked heterocyclic olefin double bonds of CBZ and oxidized it to form 10, 11-dihydro-10, 11 epoxy aminophenyl methyl ether (A, m/z = 253) with the excitation of visible light. Subsequently, the product 10-(1-amino-vinyl)-10, 10a-dihydroacridine-9-formaldehyde (B, m/z = 252) was formed due to the breaking of epoxy bonds and further transform to product C (m/z = 207) with the attack of photogenerated electrons and holes. Then product C lost CO to generate acridine D (m/z = 180), which would be further oxidized to product E (m/z = 196) [68]. In pathway II, ·OH could attack the heterorings of CBZ to form hydroxylated CBZ products F (m/z = 253) and G (m/z = 271), which were then further oxidized to product H (m/z = 267) through the ring contraction reaction, and the fracture of –CONH2 and aldehyde groups in product H could be transformed into product E (m/z = 196) [69,70]. In pathway III, CBZ molecules could be attacked by electrons and holes to produce the isomers including acridone-N-carbon aldehyde (I, m/z = 224) and 4-aldehyde-9-aromatic ketone (J, m/z = 224). Subsequently, loss of aldehyde group and ketone could generate acridine (D, m/z = 180). Under the action of ·O2 –, intermediate D could be oxidized to product E (m/z = 196). In the presence of active species, the above products would further undergo redox and ring-rupturing reactions to generate the main ring-opening products K (m/z = 137) and L (m/z = 115), which were then mineralized into CO2 and H2O. Moreover, TOC parameters of CBZ solution which could reflect mineralization degree in this reaction system were detected and it was found that the TOC removal efficiency could reach 97.60%, elucidating that most of CBZ molecules were mineralized into organic molecules.

In this work, magnetic, green and environmental-friendly photocatalyst Fe3O4/BiOBr/CQDs decorated by corncob biomass CQDs was fabricated successfully via photoinduced method. More CQDs with diameter of 5–7 nm were deposited on the (110) facet of BiOBr for FeBrCQDs-4 than FeBrCQDs-4 (NL) through EDS and TEM analyses. FeBrCQDs-4 exhibited higher photocatalytic performance on CBZ degradation, where the removal efficiencies of CBZ and TOC within 120 min visible LED light irradiation could reach 99.52% and 97.60%, respectively. On one hand, the incorporation of CQDs with unique up-conversion photoluminescence feature enhanced the light absorption ability by optical tests; on the other hand, benign design of Z-scheme structure further improved the photogenerated charge separation and transfer by photoelectrochemical experiments. ·O2 –, h+ and ·OH as the effective active species attributed to the mineralization of CBZ molecules from three dominant degradation pathways. Moreover, Fe3O4/BiOBr/HC decorated by hydrothermal corncob charcoal residues also performed excellent photocatalytic activity on CBZ degradation, but the relevant structure–activity relationship needs to be further explored in subsequent studies. In summary, novel and efficient photocatalyst Fe3O4/BiOBr/CQDs designed by biomass source CQDs and photoinduced method will have a broad application prospect in water pollution treatment in the future.
